skill.name: android-native-dev
skill.description: Android native application development and UI design guide. Covers Material Design 3, Kotlin/Compose development, project configuration, accessibility, and build troubleshooting. Read this before Android native application development.
skill.summary: Comprehensive reference guide for Android native development with Kotlin and Jetpack Compose.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: This skill is a documentation-only markdown reference guide for Android native development. The static analyzer flagged 422 patterns across 10 files, but all are false positives. The external_commands findings (265 locations) are markdown code fences showing standard Gradle build commands (e.g., ./gradlew), not actual shell execution by the skill. The weak cryptographic algorithm findings are triggered by design guideline terminology, not actual crypto code. The network, filesystem, and env_access findings point to code examples in documentation, not runtime behavior. The skill contains no executable code, no network requests, no credential handling, and no data exfiltration patterns. The only risk is that the skill instructs users to run standard Android build commands (gradlew), which is the intended and legitimate use of this development guide.

low_findings.title: External Command References in Documentation
low_findings.description: The skill documentation includes examples of Gradle build commands (./gradlew) that users may execute in their own projects. These are standard Android build tooling commands, not arbitrary shell execution. The skill itself is a markdown reference and does not execute commands directly.

content.user_title: Build Android apps with Kotlin and Compose
content.value_statement: Android development requires navigating multiple guidelines and avoiding common pitfalls. This skill provides a unified reference for Material Design 3, Kotlin best practices, Jetpack Compose patterns, accessibility requirements, and testing strategies.
actual_capabilities: Guide Kotlin and Jetpack Compose code generation following established best practices
Enforce Material Design 3 specifications for colors, typography, spacing, and components
Provide accessibility compliance guidance including content descriptions and touch targets
Assist with Gradle build configuration, product flavors, and dependency management
Recommend appropriate testing strategies for unit, instrumentation, and Compose UI tests
Identify and fix common Android build errors and configuration issues
limitations: This is a reference guide, not a code generator that builds complete projects automatically
Does not replace the Android SDK, Android Studio, or Gradle tooling
Design specifications are based on Material Design 3 and may not cover all custom design systems
Testing examples require appropriate test dependencies to be added to the project manually

use_cases.title: Start a new Android project
use_cases.description: Set up project structure, configure Gradle build files, and establish Material Design 3 styling from scratch for a new Android application.
use_cases.target_user: Junior Android developers starting their first project

use_cases.title: Migrate to Jetpack Compose
use_cases.description: Apply Compose best practices, state management patterns, and testing strategies when migrating from XML-based layouts to declarative UI.
use_cases.target_user: Mid-level developers modernizing legacy Android applications

use_cases.title: Ensure accessibility compliance
use_cases.description: Implement proper content descriptions, focus navigation, color contrast ratios, and touch target sizes to meet accessibility standards.
use_cases.target_user: Development teams preparing apps for public release

prompt_templates.title: Create a new Android screen
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a new Jetpack Compose screen for a user profile page with a profile image, name, email, and a settings button. Follow Material Design 3 guidelines.
prompt_templates.scenario: Adding a new screen to an existing Compose-based Android app

prompt_templates.title: Set up product flavors
prompt_templates.prompt: Configure Gradle product flavors for a development, staging, and production environment with different API base URLs and app names for each flavor.

prompt_templates.title: Write ViewModel tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Write unit tests for a CounterViewModel that has increment, decrement, and reset functions. Use coroutine testing with Turbine for StateFlow assertions.
prompt_templates.scenario: Testing business logic in Android ViewModels

prompt_templates.title: Implement accessible navigation
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an accessible bottom navigation bar with proper content descriptions, focus indicators, and TalkBack support. Include a checklist to verify accessibility compliance.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building an accessible navigation component for a production app

output_examples.input: Create a login screen with email and password fields, a login button, and a forgot password link using Jetpack Compose.
output: A Composable function LoginScreen with properly labeled TextField components for email and password input
A styled Button component for login with Material Design 3 theming
A TextButton for forgot password link with appropriate click handler
ViewModel integration with StateFlow for form state management
Input validation with error messaging following Material Design patterns

output_examples.input: How do I test a repository that makes network calls?
output: Use MockK to create a mock API service with coEvery for suspend functions
Apply MainDispatcherRule to replace the Main coroutine dispatcher
Write test cases verifying caching behavior with coVerify for call counts
Use Turbine to test StateFlow emissions from the repository

best_practices: Always ensure ./gradlew assembleDebug succeeds before writing business logic
Use testTag modifiers for Compose test selectors instead of text-based finders to avoid localization issues
Mark all server response data class fields as nullable to handle incomplete API responses safely
anti_patterns: Using non-null assertion operator (!!) in Kotlin, which causes crashes when values are null
Updating UI state from a background thread instead of the Main dispatcher
Using Android reserved names like background, icon, or view for custom resource identifiers

faq.question: What Android SDK version does this skill target?
faq.answer: This skill covers general Android development practices applicable to modern Android versions. Specific SDK targets should be set based on your project requirements, with a minimum SDK of 21 or higher recommended for most applications.

faq.question: Does this skill support both XML and Jetpack Compose?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill covers both traditional XML-based layouts and modern Jetpack Compose development. Compose is recommended for new development, but XML guidance is included for existing projects and migration scenarios.

faq.question: Can this skill create a new Android project from scratch?
faq.answer: This skill provides guidance for project setup including Gradle configuration, file structure, and initial dependencies. It does not automatically generate build files, but it guides you through the required steps and configuration.

faq.question: How does this skill handle Material Design 3 dynamic color?
faq.answer: The skill recommends using the Material Design 3 dynamic color system based on user wallpaper preferences. It covers the M3 color scheme tokens and how to apply them through the MaterialTheme composable.

faq.question: What testing frameworks does this skill cover?
faq.answer: The skill covers JUnit and Robolectric for local unit tests, Espresso for instrumentation tests, UI Automator for cross-app testing, and the Compose UI testing framework. It also includes Gradle Managed Devices for CI testing.

faq.question: Is this skill suitable for production applications?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidelines are based on official Android documentation, Material Design 3 specifications, Google Play quality standards, and WCAG accessibility requirements. All recommendations follow established production best practices.
